en.extract:
Tschudi's nightjar or lesser band-winged nightjar (Quechuavis decussata) is a species of nightjar in the family Caprimulgidae. It is found in Chile and Peru.


Taxonomy
Tschudi's nightjar was formally described as Caprimulgus decussatus in 1844 by the Swiss naturalist Johann Jakob von Tschudi based on a specimen collected in Peru. The specific epithet is Latin meaning "marked with crosses", from decussis meaning "ten" (X in Roman numerals).
Tschudi's nightjar has a complicated taxonomic history. It was originally considered a species, then a subspecies of band-winged nightjar (Systellura longirostris). The species was formerly placed in the genus Systellura but a 2014 molecular genetic study of the nightjars by Snorri Sigurðsson and Joel Cracraft found that the Tschudi's nightjar was only distantly related to the other member of the genus, the band-winged nightjar. Tschudi's nightjar was therefore moved to new monotypic genus, Quechuavis, that had been proposed in 2023 by Thiago Costa and coworkers. The genus name is a combination of Quechua, an indigenous people of South America, and the Latin avis meaning bird. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
Tschudi's nightjar is 20 to 21 cm (7.9 to 8.3 in) long. Males weigh 28.5 to 35 g (1.0 to 1.2 oz) and females about 32 g (1.1 oz). It is overall grayish brown with blackish speckles. The male has a broad tawny or cinnamon collar on the hindneck, a small white patch on the throat, white bands on the wing, and white bands and tips on the tail. The female's throat is buff, the bands on the wing are buff, and the tail usually has no white.


Distribution and habitat
Tschudi's nightjar is found along most of western Peru and extreme northern Chile. It inhabits the littoral and foothills in this arid landscape. It generally keeps to open country, clearings, and wooded edges though it is also found in urban areas including Lima, Peru. In Peru it ranges from sea level to 1,300 m (4,300 ft) but has been reported as high as 3,350 m (11,000 ft) in Chile.


Behavior


Feeding
Nothing is known about Tschudi's nightjar's diet or feeding behavior.


Breeding
The breeding season of Tschudi's nightjar is thought to be from November or earlier to January. Essentially nothing else is known about its breeding biology.


Vocalization
Tschudi's nightjar's song is "a loud series of well-defined, but slightly buzzy 'cueeo' notes". Its alarm call is "a slightly squeaky-sounding 'wick'".


Status
The IUCN has assessed Tschudi's nightjar as being of Least Concern. It has a range estimated at 472,000 km2 (182,000 sq mi), and though its population has not been quantified it is thought to be stable.

'''Tschudi's nightjar''' or '''lesser band-winged nightjar'''<ref name=HBW8.1>HBW and BirdLife International (2024). Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 8.1. Available at: https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/taxonomy retrieved August 26, 2024</ref> ('''''Quechuavis decussata''''') is a species of [[nightjar]] in the family [[Caprimulgidae]]. It is found in [[Chile]] and [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 14.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=August 2024 |access-date=August 19, 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
Tschudi's nightjar was [[species description|formally described]] as ''Caprimulgus decussatus'' in 1844 by the Swiss naturalist [[Johann Jakob von Tschudi]] based on a specimen collected in Peru.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Tschudi | first=Johann Jakob von | author-link=Johann Jakob von Tschudi | year=1844 | title=Avium conspectus quae in Republica Peruana reperiuntur et pleraeque observatae vel collectae sunt in itinere | language=Latin | journal=Archiv für Naturgeschichte | volume=10 | issue=1 | page=262–317 [268] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13704171 }}</ref><ref name=peters>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=200 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476671 }}</ref> The specific epithet is [[Latin]] meaning "marked with crosses", from ''decussis'' meaning "ten" (X in Roman numerals).<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=decussatus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=decussatus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=10 November 2025}}</ref>

Tschudi's nightjar has a complicated taxonomic history. It was originally considered a species, then a subspecies of [[band-winged nightjar]] (''Systellura longirostris'').<ref name=peters/> The species was formerly placed in the genus ''[[Systellura]]'' but a 2014 molecular genetic study of the nightjars by Snorri Sigurðsson and Joel Cracraft found that the Tschudi's nightjar was only distantly related to the other member of the genus, the [[band-winged nightjar]].<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Sigurðsson | first1=Snorri | last2=Cracraft | first2=Joel | date=2014 | title=Deciphering the diversity and history of New World nightjars (Aves: Caprimulgidae) using molecular phylogenetics | journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society | volume=170 | issue=3 | pages=506-545 | doi=10.1111/zoj.12109 | doi-access=free}}</ref> Tschudi's nightjar was therefore moved to new [[monotypic]] genus, ''Quechuavis'', that had been proposed in 2023 by Thiago Costa and coworkers. The genus name is a combination of [[Quechua people|Quechua]], an indigenous people of South America, and the [[Latin]] ''avis'' meaning bird.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Costa | first1=T.V.V. | last2=van Els | first2=P. | last3=Braun | first3=M.J. | last4=Whitney | first4=B.M. | last5=Cleere | first5=N. | last6=Sigurosson | first6=S. | last7=Silveira | first7=L.F. | date=2023 | title=Systematic revision and generic classification of a clade of New World nightjars (Caprimulgidae), with descriptions of new genera from South America | journal=Avian Systematics | volume=1 | issue=6 | pages=55-99 | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374615773 }}</ref><ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=10 November 2025 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no subspecies are recognised.<ref name=avilist/>

==Description==

Tschudi's nightjar is {{convert|20|to|21|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. Males weigh {{convert|28.5|to|35|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} and females about {{convert|32|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It is overall grayish brown with blackish speckles. The male has a broad tawny or cinnamon collar on the hindneck, a small white patch on the throat, white bands on the wing, and white bands and tips on the tail. The female's throat is buff, the bands on the wing are buff, and the tail usually has no white.<ref name=TSNI-BOW>del Hoyo, J., N. Collar, and G. M. Kirwan (2024). Tschudi's Nightjar (''Quechuavis decussata''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.bawnig3.01.1 retrieved December 3, 2024</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

Tschudi's nightjar is found along most of western Peru and extreme northern Chile. It inhabits the littoral and foothills in this arid landscape. It generally keeps to open country, clearings, and wooded edges though it is also found in urban areas including Lima, Peru. In Peru it ranges from sea level to {{convert|1300|m|ft|abbr=on}} but has been reported as high as {{convert|3350|m|ft|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} in Chile.<ref name=TSNI-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===

Nothing is known about Tschudi's nightjar's diet or feeding behavior.<ref name=TSNI-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The breeding season of Tschudi's nightjar is thought to be from November or earlier to January. Essentially nothing else is known about its breeding biology.<ref name=TSNI-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Systellura-decussata |species=Tschudi's nightjar}}
===Vocalization===

Tschudi's nightjar's song is "a loud series of well-defined, but slightly buzzy 'cueeo' notes". Its alarm call is "a slightly squeaky-sounding 'wick'".<ref name=TSNI-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed Tschudi's nightjar as being of Least Concern. It has a range estimated at {{convert|472000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, and though its population has not been quantified it is thought to be stable.<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=TSNI-BOW/>
